messages: 330001	Introduction to the New Testament Canon and Historical Context	# Introduction to the New Testament Canon and Historical Context

The New Testament Canon represents a critical focal point in Christian theology and biblical studies, serving as the foundational corpus that articulates the life, teachings, and redemptive work of Jesus Christ and the subsequent formation of the early church. Theologically, the canon functions as the culmination of the divine narrative that begins in the Old Testament, fulfilling its promises and prophecies. It offers a coherent and comprehensive revelation of God's salvific plan, encapsulating doctrines central to Christian faith, including Christology, soteriology, ecclesiology, and eschatology. The New Testament’s theological significance is profound, as it grounds the church’s understanding of the person and work of Christ, the nature of salvation, and the call to discipleship and mission.

In systematic theology, the New Testament canon is not an isolated compilation but is intrinsically linked 	Matthew 5:17-20; Matthew 16:13-20; Luke 1:1-4; John 1:1-18; Acts 1:1-11; Acts 15:1-21; Romans 3:21-26; 1 Corinthians 15:1-11; Galatians 1:6-10; Ephesians 2:11-22; Philippians 2:5-11; Colossians 1:15-20; 2 Timothy 3:14-17; Hebrews 1:1-4; Revelation 1:1-8

330001	Introduction to the New Testament Canon and Historical Context	# Introduction to the New Testament Canon and Historical Context

## I. Introduction

The New Testament Canon represents a critical focal point in Christian theology and biblical studies, serving as the foundational corpus that articulates the life, teachings, and redemptive work of Jesus Christ and the subsequent formation of the early church. Theologically, the canon functions as the culmination of the divine narrative that begins in the Old Testament, fulfilling its promises and prophecies. It offers a coherent and comprehensive revelation of God's salvific plan, encapsulating doctrines central to Christian faith, including Christology, soteriology, ecclesiology, and eschatology. The New Testament’s theological significance is profound, as it grounds the church’s understanding of the person and work of Christ, the nature of salvation, and the call to discipleship and mission.

In systematic theology, the New Testament canon is not an isolated compilation but is intrinsically linked 	Matthew 5:17-20; Matthew 16:13-20; Luke 1:1-4; John 1:1-18; Acts 1:1-11; Acts 15:1-21; Romans 3:21-26; 1 Corinthians 15:1-11; Galatians 1:6-10; Ephesians 2:11-22; Philippians 2:5-11; Colossians 1:15-20; 2 Timothy 3:14-17; Hebrews 1:1-4; Revelation 1:1-8
